tickets.summary: DB connection pool exhaustion on order-service causing 500 errors
tickets.description: Prometheus alert fired: order-service DB connection pool utilization at 100%. Order creation API returning 500 errors. Error logs show 'Cannot acquire connection from pool - timeout after 30000ms'. Approximately 12% of checkout requests failing. Grafana dashboard shows pool exhaustion started at 03:18 UTC correlating with a spike in bulk order processing jobs.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 03:18 UTC: DB pool utilization crosses 90% threshold on order-service pods
- 03:22 UTC: PagerDuty alert fires, on-call (Alice) paged
- 03:24 UTC: Alice acknowledges, begins investigation. Confirms 500 errors on /api/orders endpoint via Grafana RED dashboard
- 03:26 UTC: Identifies connection pool at 20/20 active connections across all pods. Normal baseline is 8-12.
- 03:28 UTC: Reviews recent deployments — v2.14.3 deployed at 22:00 previous day. Checks diff: bulk_update() method in OrderBatchProcessor was refactored
- 03:30 UTC: Found root cause — bulk_update() opens a new DB connection per item in batch instead of reusing a single connection. Connection.close() is in a finally block that only executes after the entire batch, not per-item. Under load, connections leak when batch processing takes longer than pool timeout.
- 03:32 UTC: Rolled back order-service to v2.14.2 via ArgoCD
- 03:35 UTC: Pool utilization drops to normal. 500 errors stop. Incident resolved.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why were orders failing? DB connection pool exhausted.
2. Why was the pool exhausted? bulk_update() was leaking connections.
3. Why was it leaking? Refactored code acquired a connection per loop iteration but only released in outer finally block.
4. Why wasn't this caught? Unit tests mock the DB layer; integration tests don't run concurrent batch workloads.
5. Why no concurrent integration tests? Test environment lacks production-like load testing for batch paths.

Fix Applied: Rolled back to v2.14.2. Follow-up PR #1847 fixes the connection handling in bulk_update() using a context manager pattern.

Lessons Learned:
- Need connection pool utilization alerts at 80% (currently only at 95%)
- pgbouncer deployment would have mitigated this (tracked in INFRA-445)
- Batch processing code paths need integration tests with concurrency

tickets.summary: DB connection pool exhaustion during flash sale event
tickets.description: Flash sale started at 14:00 UTC. Order-service began returning 503 errors within 5 minutes. DB connection pool fully saturated. All 20 connections in use with long wait queues. Customer-facing impact: checkout completely broken for approximately 22 minutes during peak sale event.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 14:00 UTC: Black Friday flash sale goes live. Traffic spikes 8x normal.
- 14:03 UTC: order-service latency climbs from 120ms p99 to 2400ms p99
- 14:05 UTC: PagerDuty SEV-1 alert fires. Connection pool at 100%. On-call Bob paged.
- 14:07 UTC: Bob joins war room. Confirms pool saturation on all 4 order-service pods (20 connections each, 80 total against PostgreSQL).
- 14:10 UTC: PostgreSQL max_connections is 200. With other services, headroom is limited. Bob increases order-service pool to 30 per pod via ConfigMap hot-reload.
- 14:14 UTC: Pool increase helps but PostgreSQL now at 180/200 connections. Latency improves but still elevated.
- 14:18 UTC: Scaled order-service to 6 pods with pool=20 each. This exceeds PostgreSQL capacity. Rolled back to 4 pods with pool=30.
- 14:22 UTC: Added read replicas to handle SELECT queries. Redirected order history and status checks to replica.
- 14:27 UTC: Latency returns to acceptable levels. Error rate drops below 0.1%. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Connection pool size of 20 per pod was sized for normal traffic (3x headroom). Flash sale drove 8x traffic with no advance capacity planning for the DB tier. No connection pooler (pgbouncer) in front of PostgreSQL to multiplex connections.

Fix Applied: Temporarily increased pool to 30. Long-term action items created for pgbouncer deployment and capacity planning for sale events.

Lessons Learned:
- Must do capacity planning for scheduled high-traffic events
- pgbouncer is critical — direct connections to PostgreSQL don't scale
- Need a flash-sale runbook with pre-scaling steps
- order-service should have separate read/write connection pools

tickets.summary: DB pool exhaustion after schema migration on order-service
tickets.description: Following a scheduled schema migration at 07:30 UTC, order-service DB connection pool slowly climbed to 100% over 40 minutes. New reporting query introduced in the migration was opening direct JDBC connections bypassing the HikariCP pool. Alerts fired when pool hit 95%.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 07:30 UTC: Scheduled migration deployed (order-service v2.11.0) with new reporting tables and a background analytics job.
- 07:45 UTC: Pool utilization begins slow climb. No immediate alerts (threshold at 95%).
- 08:00 UTC: Grafana shows pool at 72%, up from typical 35%. No one monitoring post-migration dashboard.
- 08:12 UTC: Alert fires at 95%. On-call Carol paged.
- 08:18 UTC: Carol confirms pool exhaustion. Reviews migration changelog.
- 08:25 UTC: Identifies new ReportingAnalyticsJob class. It uses DriverManager.getConnection() directly instead of the Spring DataSource (HikariCP). Each scheduled run opens 2 connections that are never returned to the pool.
- 08:32 UTC: Job runs every 5 minutes, leaking 2 connections per run. Over 40 minutes, 16 connections leaked, filling the 20-connection pool.
- 08:38 UTC: Disabled the analytics job via feature flag.
- 08:42 UTC: Restarted order-service pods to reclaim leaked connections.
- 08:57 UTC: Pool stable at normal levels. Incident resolved.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why pool exhaustion? Connections leaked over time.
2. Why leaked? ReportingAnalyticsJob opened raw JDBC connections outside the pool.
3. Why raw JDBC? Developer copied a standalone script pattern into the Spring service.
4. Why not caught in review? Reviewer didn't flag the DriverManager usage.
5. Why no automated check? No static analysis rule for direct JDBC connection creation.

Fix Applied: Disabled analytics job. PR #1602 refactored it to use the DataSource bean. Added a Checkstyle rule to flag DriverManager.getConnection() usage.

Lessons Learned:
- Add linting rules to prevent direct JDBC connections in Spring services
- Post-migration monitoring window should be mandatory (added to SOP-002)
- Pool utilization alert threshold lowered to 80%

tickets.summary: Payment gateway timeout cascade causing order failures
tickets.description: Payment provider (Stripe) experiencing elevated latency (p99 > 30s vs normal 800ms). Payment-service HTTP client threads blocked waiting on responses. Thread pool exhaustion cascaded to order-service which could not process checkouts. All checkout attempts failing with 504 Gateway Timeout.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 19:40 UTC: Stripe status page reports degraded API performance.
- 19:43 UTC: payment-service p99 latency jumps from 800ms to 28s.
- 19:45 UTC: PagerDuty SEV-1 fires. On-call Dave paged. order-service returning 504s on checkout.
- 19:48 UTC: Dave confirms payment-service threads exhausted. All 200 HTTP client threads blocked on Stripe calls.
- 19:52 UTC: Checked for circuit breaker — none configured on the Stripe client. Payment-service has a 60s socket timeout, far too high for synchronous checkout flow.
- 19:55 UTC: Applied emergency config change: reduced Stripe client timeout to 5s. This caused fast failures instead of thread blocking.
- 19:58 UTC: Thread pool recovers. But now all payments failing fast with timeout errors.
- 20:02 UTC: Enabled fallback payment processor (Adyen) via feature flag for new transactions.
- 20:08 UTC: Checkout flow restored via Adyen. Existing Stripe-initiated transactions queued for retry.
- 20:15 UTC: Stripe reports recovery. Switched primary back to Stripe.
- 20:20 UTC: All metrics nominal. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Stripe experienced a partial outage with severely degraded latency. payment-service had no circuit breaker and a 60s timeout on the HTTP client, causing thread pool exhaustion. The cascade hit order-service because it makes synchronous calls to payment-service during checkout.

Fix Applied: Reduced timeout to 5s, activated fallback processor. Follow-up: PR #1923 adds Resilience4j circuit breaker on Stripe client with 50% failure threshold, 30s open duration. PR #1925 makes the fallback processor automatic.

Lessons Learned:
- All external service calls must have circuit breakers (ARCH decision logged)
- Payment-service needs a fallback provider that activates automatically
- 60s default timeout was never reviewed — need timeout audit across all services
- Checkout should degrade gracefully (queue payment, confirm order) rather than fail entirely

tickets.summary: Payment service OOMKilled repeatedly in production
tickets.description: Kubernetes OOMKilled events on payment-service pods. Pods restarting every 15-20 minutes. Memory usage climbing linearly from startup. JVM heap dumps show large number of unreleased Apache HttpClient connection objects.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 11:25 UTC: First pod OOMKilled. Kubernetes restarts it automatically.
- 11:30 UTC: Second pod killed. PagerDuty alert fires for repeated restarts. On-call Eve paged.
- 11:33 UTC: Eve checks pod events: OOMKilled with memory at container limit (512Mi). JVM heap set to 384m.
- 11:36 UTC: Takes heap dump from surviving pod before it dies. Analyzes with Eclipse MAT.
- 11:39 UTC: Heap dominated by org.apache.http.impl.conn.PoolingHttpClientConnectionManager — thousands of stale connection objects. The HTTP client for webhook delivery was creating new HttpClient instances per request instead of reusing a singleton.
- 11:42 UTC: Each HttpClient allocates a connection pool. Under load, hundreds of pools created, each holding connection buffers.
- 11:44 UTC: Rolled back to payment-service v3.8.1 (previous version used a singleton HttpClient).
- 11:48 UTC: Pods stable. Memory usage flat at ~280Mi. Incident resolved.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why OOMKilled? Container exceeded 512Mi memory limit.
2. Why high memory? Thousands of HttpClient connection pool objects in heap.
3. Why so many pools? New HttpClient created per webhook delivery request.
4. Why per-request creation? Refactoring in v3.9.0 accidentally moved HttpClient construction inside the request handler method.
5. Why not caught? Load tests run with 10 concurrent users; production sees 500+ concurrent webhook deliveries.

Fix Applied: Rolled back to v3.8.1. PR #1876 restores singleton HttpClient with proper connection pool configuration. Added unit test asserting HttpClient is a singleton bean.

Lessons Learned:
- Need memory limit alerts before OOM (alert at 80% of limit)
- Load tests must simulate production concurrency levels
- Consider using Spring WebClient (non-blocking) for webhook delivery

tickets.summary: Payment service high GC pause times causing transaction timeouts
tickets.description: Payment-service experiencing GC pauses exceeding 5 seconds. Transaction success rate dropped from 99.8% to 87%. JVM old generation repeatedly filling up. Grafana JVM dashboard shows full GC events every 45 seconds.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 16:05 UTC: Grafana alert for payment-service p99 latency > 5s.
- 16:10 UTC: PagerDuty fires. On-call Dave paged.
- 16:13 UTC: Dave checks JVM dashboard. Old gen at 98%, full GC every 45s with 5-8s pause times. G1GC failing to keep up.
- 16:17 UTC: Checks recent deployments. v3.7.0 deployed at 14:00 UTC with a new feature: caching payment provider responses for idempotency.
- 16:20 UTC: The PaymentResponseCache uses a ConcurrentHashMap with no TTL or size bound. Every Stripe API response (avg 12KB JSON) cached indefinitely.
- 16:23 UTC: After 2 hours of production traffic, cache holds ~50,000 entries consuming ~600MB. This exceeds old gen capacity.
- 16:26 UTC: Disabled the response caching via feature flag (LaunchDarkly: payment-response-cache = false).
- 16:30 UTC: Triggered manual GC. Old gen dropped to 22%. GC pauses returned to <200ms.
- 16:38 UTC: All metrics nominal for 8 minutes. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Unbounded in-memory cache for payment responses. ConcurrentHashMap grew without limit, filling the JVM old generation and causing stop-the-world GC pauses.

Fix Applied: Disabled feature flag. PR #1789 replaces ConcurrentHashMap with Caffeine cache: max 10,000 entries, 5-minute TTL, estimated max memory 120MB.

Lessons Learned:
- All in-memory caches MUST have TTL and size bounds (added to coding standards)
- JVM old gen utilization needs alerting (added at 75% threshold)
- Feature flags saved us — new features should always be behind flags
- Consider Redis for caching instead of in-process memory

tickets.summary: Cart service Redis connection pool exhaustion
tickets.description: Cart service returning errors on all cart operations (add/remove/view). Logs show 'redis.clients.jedis.exceptions.JedisExhaustedPoolException: Could not get a resource from the pool'. Redis server healthy, but client-side connection pool at maximum.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 09:12 UTC: Error rate on cart-service spikes to 40%.
- 09:15 UTC: PagerDuty alert fires. On-call Frank paged.
- 09:17 UTC: Frank confirms Redis is healthy (memory 45%, CPU 12%). Problem is client-side.
- 09:19 UTC: Jedis pool config: maxTotal=8 (default!). With 3 pods, that's 24 total connections. Morning traffic peak hitting 800 req/s on cart operations.
- 09:21 UTC: Each cart operation takes ~5ms on Redis. At 800 req/s with 24 connections and 5ms per op, theoretical max throughput is 4800 req/s. But some operations (cart merge on login) take 50ms, reducing effective capacity.
- 09:23 UTC: Applied ConfigMap update: maxTotal=32, maxIdle=16, minIdle=8. Triggered rolling restart.
- 09:27 UTC: New pods coming up with updated pool config.
- 09:30 UTC: All pods rolled. Error rate at 0%. Pool utilization at 35%. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Jedis connection pool was left at default size (maxTotal=8) when cart-service was originally deployed. Morning traffic growth over the past month exceeded the pool's capacity. Cart merge operations (50ms) during login rush hour created contention.

Fix Applied: Increased pool to maxTotal=32, maxIdle=16, minIdle=8. PR #1945 also adds connection pool metrics to Prometheus exporter.

Lessons Learned:
- Default connection pool configs must be reviewed before production deployment
- All connection pools need Prometheus metrics exported
- Need capacity model for Redis connection pools based on traffic patterns
- Cart merge operation should be optimized (pipeline Redis commands)

tickets.summary: Cart service Redis OOM — server evicting keys
tickets.description: ElastiCache Redis instance for cart-service hit maxmemory limit (2GB). Eviction policy (allkeys-lru) began evicting active cart sessions. Customers reporting empty carts mid-checkout. Redis memory usage graph shows linear growth over past 2 weeks.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 20:28 UTC: Customer complaints via support: carts emptied during checkout.
- 20:33 UTC: Engineering paged. On-call Frank investigates.
- 20:36 UTC: Redis memory at 2.0GB/2.0GB. Evictions spiking. INFO command shows 4.2 million keys.
- 20:40 UTC: Normal cart session count should be ~200K (active sessions). Investigated key patterns.
- 20:43 UTC: Found millions of keys matching pattern `cart:anonymous:*` with no TTL set. Anonymous browsing sessions were creating cart entries that persisted forever.
- 20:46 UTC: The cart-service code sets TTL on authenticated user carts (24h) but anonymous cart creation path (added in v1.12.0, 3 weeks ago) omits the EXPIRE call.
- 20:50 UTC: Scaled Redis to 4GB (ElastiCache resize) to stop evictions immediately.
- 20:55 UTC: Ran cleanup script to delete anonymous cart keys older than 2 hours. Removed 3.8 million keys.
- 21:05 UTC: Memory dropped to 800MB. Evictions stopped.
- 21:13 UTC: Deployed hotfix v1.12.1 adding TTL (2 hours) to anonymous carts. Incident resolved.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why were carts disappearing? Redis evicting keys under memory pressure.
2. Why memory pressure? 4.2M keys instead of expected 200K.
3. Why so many keys? Anonymous cart sessions stored without TTL.
4. Why no TTL? Code path for anonymous carts missed the EXPIRE call.
5. Why not caught? No monitoring on Redis key count growth rate; code review didn't flag the missing TTL.

Fix Applied: Scaled Redis, cleaned stale keys, deployed TTL fix. PR #1868 adds TTL to all cart key creation paths.

Lessons Learned:
- ALL Redis keys must have TTL — enforce via code review checklist
- Alert on Redis memory growth rate, not just absolute threshold
- Alert on key count anomalies
- Anonymous session data needs aggressive TTL (2h max)
- Consider separate Redis instances for session data vs cache data

tickets.summary: Notification queue backlog — emails and SMS delayed 45+ minutes
tickets.description: RabbitMQ queue 'notifications.email' has 85,000 messages pending (normal: <500). Queue 'notifications.sms' has 12,000 pending. Consumers connected but not processing. Order confirmation emails delayed by 45+ minutes.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 07:15 UTC: RabbitMQ dashboard shows notifications.email queue depth at 85K, growing.
- 07:20 UTC: Alert fires. On-call Grace paged.
- 07:23 UTC: Grace checks consumer status. 3 consumers connected to email queue but prefetch count shows 0 messages being processed.
- 07:26 UTC: Thread dump on notification-service pods reveals all consumer threads in BLOCKED state. Deadlock between EmailTemplateEngine.render() and TemplateCache.refresh().
- 07:29 UTC: The TemplateCache.refresh() method acquires a write lock and calls EmailTemplateEngine.render() for validation, while render() acquires a read lock on the cache. Under concurrent access, this creates a deadlock.
- 07:32 UTC: Restarted all notification-service pods to clear the deadlock.
- 07:35 UTC: Consumers resume processing. Queue draining at ~3000 msg/s.
- 07:40 UTC: Queue depth below 1000. Processing caught up within 10 minutes. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Classic lock-ordering deadlock in notification-service. TemplateCache.refresh() holds writeLock and calls render(), which tries to acquire readLock. Under concurrent load, thread A holds writeLock waiting for readLock, thread B holds readLock waiting for writeLock.

Fix Applied: Restarted pods. PR #1831 refactors to use a copy-on-write pattern for the template cache, eliminating the deadlock.

Lessons Learned:
- Need consumer lag alerts (queue depth) with shorter thresholds
- Consumer processing rate should be monitored (msgs/sec dropping to 0 is a clear signal)
- Deadlock detection: add periodic thread dump analysis or use JVM deadlock detection MBean
- Template cache refresh should happen out-of-band, not inline with message processing

tickets.summary: Catalog search returning empty results after Elasticsearch upgrade
tickets.description: Product search on shopfast.com returning zero results for all queries. Catalog browse pages showing empty product listings. Elasticsearch cluster upgraded from 7.17 to 8.11 at 11:00 UTC as scheduled maintenance. Search functionality broke approximately 45 minutes after upgrade completion.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 11:00 UTC: Scheduled Elasticsearch upgrade from 7.17 to 8.11 begins.
- 11:30 UTC: Upgrade completes. Initial smoke tests pass (index exists, cluster health green).
- 11:45 UTC: Automated reindexing job triggered by catalog-service detects version change.
- 12:00 UTC: Reindexing job fails silently. New ES 8.x client rejects the index mapping due to deprecated 'type' field in mapping definition. Error logged at WARN level, not ERROR.
- 12:05 UTC: Customer reports of empty search results. PagerDuty fires. On-call Henry paged.
- 12:10 UTC: Henry confirms: search API returns {results: [], total: 0} for all queries. ES cluster healthy.
- 12:15 UTC: Checks index: product_catalog index has 0 documents. Old index was deleted during reindex job initialization.
- 12:20 UTC: Finds WARN log about mapping compatibility. The reindex job deletes the old index first, then creates new one. Creation failed due to mapping error, leaving an empty index.
- 12:30 UTC: Attempts to fix mapping and reindex from PostgreSQL source of truth.
- 12:40 UTC: Reindexing in progress. 450K products to index.
- 12:55 UTC: Reindexing complete. Search returning results.
- 13:00 UTC: All search metrics normal. Incident resolved. Total customer impact: 55 minutes of broken search.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why empty search results? Product catalog index had 0 documents.
2. Why 0 documents? Reindex job deleted old index, then failed to create new one.
3. Why did creation fail? ES 8.x rejected the mapping with deprecated 'type' field.
4. Why wasn't mapping updated? Upgrade runbook didn't include application compatibility checks.
5. Why did the job delete before confirming new index? Reindex implementation uses destructive delete-then-create instead of safe alias-swap pattern.

Fix Applied: Fixed mapping for ES 8.x compatibility. Reindexed from PostgreSQL. PR #1678 refactors reindexing to use alias-swap pattern (create new index, populate, swap alias, delete old).

Lessons Learned:
- Elasticsearch upgrades need application-level compatibility testing, not just cluster health
- Reindexing must use alias-swap pattern, never delete-then-create
- WARN-level logs for index creation failures should be ERROR
- Need a search quality canary that alerts when result counts drop to zero
- Upgrade runbook updated with pre-upgrade mapping validation step

tickets.summary: API gateway returning 429 Too Many Requests to all users
tickets.description: All API requests being rejected with HTTP 429. Rate limiting applied globally instead of per-user. Website and mobile app completely non-functional. Caused by a configuration deployment 5 minutes prior.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 14:57 UTC: Config change deployed to api-gateway rate limiter via ArgoCD. Intent: reduce per-user rate limit from 100/min to 60/min.
- 15:00 UTC: 429 error rate spikes to 95% across all endpoints.
- 15:02 UTC: PagerDuty SEV-1. On-call Alice paged.
- 15:04 UTC: Alice confirms 429 on all requests. Checks recent changes — rate limit config deployed 5 min ago.
- 15:06 UTC: Reviews the config diff. The YAML change set `rate_limit.scope: global` instead of `rate_limit.scope: per_user`. This means the 60/min limit applies to ALL traffic combined, not per user.
- 15:08 UTC: Reverts the config change via ArgoCD rollback.
- 15:10 UTC: api-gateway pods pick up reverted config. 429 rate drops immediately.
- 15:12 UTC: Error rate at 0%. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Configuration error in rate limiter. The `scope` field was changed from `per_user` to `global` inadvertently. The config PR was approved without testing in staging. The 60 req/min global limit was hit instantly by aggregate production traffic.

Fix Applied: Rolled back config. PR #1972 adds config validation that rejects `scope: global` with limits below 10,000/min. Also adds a staging deployment requirement for rate limit changes.

Lessons Learned:
- Rate limiter config changes need mandatory staging validation
- Add a guardrail: global rate limits below a threshold should require explicit override
- Config-only changes are just as risky as code changes — same review rigor needed
- MTTR was fast (10min) because the config change was recent and easy to correlate

tickets.summary: Inventory service slow queries causing timeout cascade
tickets.description: Inventory lookups timing out. PostgreSQL slow query log shows full table scans on inventory.stock_levels table (12M rows) for SKU-based lookups. Query time >8s per lookup. Cascading to catalog-service and order-service which depend on inventory checks.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 10:10 UTC: catalog-service latency increases. Product pages loading slowly.
- 10:15 UTC: Alert fires for inventory-service p99 > 5s. On-call Carol paged.
- 10:18 UTC: Carol checks inventory-service metrics. DB query time for /api/inventory/sku/{sku} endpoint averaging 8.2s.
- 10:22 UTC: PostgreSQL pg_stat_statements shows: SELECT * FROM stock_levels WHERE sku = $1 doing sequential scan. Table has 12M rows.
- 10:25 UTC: Checks indexes: no index on stock_levels.sku column. The table was created 2 months ago during inventory service rewrite. The CREATE INDEX statement was in the migration but commented out with a TODO note.
- 10:28 UTC: Creates the index: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_stock_levels_sku ON inventory.stock_levels(sku).
- 10:35 UTC: Index creation complete. Query time drops from 8.2s to 3ms.
- 10:40 UTC: All dependent services recovered. Latency nominal. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Missing index on stock_levels.sku column. The index was planned during schema design but left as a TODO comment in the migration script. Sequential scans on a 12M row table caused 8s query times.

Fix Applied: Created the missing index. PR #1856 also adds indexes on warehouse_id and last_updated columns. Added a CI check that flags commented-out DDL statements in migrations.

Lessons Learned:
- Migration scripts should never have commented-out DDL — either include it or track in a ticket
- Need slow query monitoring with alerting (pg_stat_statements integration)
- Inventory table grew from 500K to 12M rows — query plan changed from index scan to seq scan at scale
- Consider EXPLAIN ANALYZE in CI for critical query paths

tickets.summary: Checkout latency spike — payment gateway degradation with no circuit breaker
tickets.description: Checkout flow latency spiked to 25s p99 (normal: 1.2s). Payment gateway (Stripe) responding with elevated latency (~8s per call vs normal 400ms). No circuit breaker on payment-service to Stripe integration. order-service synchronous call to payment-service blocking request threads.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 22:05 UTC: Stripe status page shows elevated API latency in EU region.
- 22:08 UTC: payment-service p99 climbs to 12s. order-service checkout p99 at 25s.
- 22:10 UTC: Alert fires. On-call Eve paged.
- 22:14 UTC: Eve confirms Stripe degradation. payment-service has no circuit breaker (known tech debt from OPS-1301 — circuit breaker PR was approved but not yet deployed).
- 22:18 UTC: Checked: PR #1923 (circuit breaker) was merged but stuck in staging. Promotes to production via ArgoCD.
- 22:24 UTC: payment-service v3.12.0 rolling out with Resilience4j circuit breaker.
- 22:28 UTC: Circuit breaker opens after detecting 50% failure rate. Fast-fails payment calls in 50ms instead of waiting 8s.
- 22:32 UTC: Enabled fallback payment response: queues payment for retry, returns provisional order confirmation to customer.
- 22:35 UTC: Checkout latency drops to 1.8s p99. Customers can complete orders.
- 22:40 UTC: Stripe recovers. Circuit breaker closes. Normal payment flow resumes. Queued payments processed. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Stripe API degradation combined with missing circuit breaker in production. The circuit breaker fix from OPS-1301 was merged but not promoted to production for 12 days.

Fix Applied: Deployed the circuit breaker to production. Added fallback payment queueing.

Lessons Learned:
- Critical fixes must be deployed to production within 48 hours of merge
- Need a dashboard tracking merged-but-not-deployed PRs
- Circuit breaker pattern validated — reduced MTTR significantly vs OPS-1301
- Provisional order confirmation with queued payment is a viable degradation strategy

tickets.description: All order-service pods entering CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl describe shows OOMKilled. Container memory limit set to 256Mi but JVM heap alone configured at 256m. No headroom for JVM metaspace, thread stacks, or native memory.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 06:40 UTC: order-service pods begin crashing. Kubernetes restarts trigger CrashLoopBackOff.
- 06:45 UTC: PagerDuty fires. On-call Bob paged.
- 06:47 UTC: Bob checks: kubectl get pods -n shopfast-prod shows all 4 order-service pods in CrashLoopBackOff. Last state: OOMKilled.
- 06:49 UTC: kubectl describe pod shows container memory limit: 256Mi. Checks JVM flags in deployment: -Xmx256m -Xms256m.
- 06:50 UTC: The JVM needs ~80-100MB beyond heap for metaspace, thread stacks, direct buffers, and GC overhead. Setting heap equal to container limit guarantees OOM.
- 06:51 UTC: Checks git log — deployment manifest updated yesterday in PR #1901 which reduced memory limit from 512Mi to 256Mi as part of 'resource optimization'. JVM flags not updated to match.
- 06:53 UTC: Patches deployment: increases memory limit back to 512Mi. kubectl apply triggers rolling restart.
- 06:55 UTC: Pods starting with 512Mi limit. JVM starts successfully.
- 06:57 UTC: All pods running. Order processing resumes. Incident resolved.

Root Cause: Container memory limit reduced from 512Mi to 256Mi without adjusting JVM heap settings. JVM heap at 256m plus ~90MB JVM overhead exceeded the 256Mi container limit on startup.

Fix Applied: Restored 512Mi limit. PR #1910 sets proper values: container limit 512Mi, -Xmx384m -Xms384m, leaving 128Mi for JVM overhead.

Lessons Learned:
- JVM container memory formula: limit = heap + 128Mi overhead minimum
- Resource limit changes require application-awareness (JVM, Go, Python all different)
- Add OPA policy to reject JVM deployments where Xmx >= container limit
- PR #1901 review should have caught the mismatch — add CI check for JVM memory validation

tickets.summary: Cross-service outage during deployment — bad config pushed to all services
tickets.description: All 7 microservices experiencing errors simultaneously. Common config shared via shopfast-common ConfigMap was updated with invalid database connection string format. ArgoCD synced the change to all services within a 5-minute window. Services began failing on next DB connection attempt.
tickets.resolution_notes:
Timeline:
- 17:20 UTC: Platform team pushes update to shopfast-common ConfigMap. Change intended to rotate DB password. The new connection string uses an invalid format: postgres://user:p@ss@host (unescaped @ in password).
- 17:22 UTC: ArgoCD auto-sync enabled. Begins rolling update of all services.
- 17:25 UTC: api-gateway picks up new config first. Starts failing health checks.
- 17:27 UTC: catalog-service, cart-service pick up config. Errors cascade.
- 17:30 UTC: PagerDuty SEV-1 fires — 5 services simultaneously unhealthy. On-call Alice paged.
- 17:33 UTC: Alice joins war room. Sees all services failing. Checks for common factor.
- 17:36 UTC: All error logs show 'Failed to create connection pool: invalid connection URL'. Identifies shopfast-common ConfigMap as the source.
- 17:40 UTC: Attempts to rollback ConfigMap. ArgoCD history shows previous version. Initiates rollback.
- 17:45 UTC: Rollback applied. But services don't auto-restart — they cached the broken config.
- 17:48 UTC: Triggers rolling restart of all services: kubectl rollout restart deployment -n shopfast-prod --all.
- 17:55 UTC: Services starting up with corrected config. api-gateway healthy first.
- 18:05 UTC: 5 of 7 services healthy. notification-service and inventory-service slower due to startup initialization.
- 18:15 UTC: All 7 services healthy.
- 18:20 UTC: All metrics nominal for 5 minutes. Incident resolved.

Root Cause (5 Whys):
1. Why did all services fail? Shared ConfigMap had invalid DB connection string.
2. Why was the string invalid? Special character in password not URL-encoded.
3. Why wasn't it validated? No config validation step in the deployment pipeline.
4. Why did it affect all services simultaneously? ArgoCD auto-sync with no staged rollout for shared configs.
5. Why auto-sync on shared configs? Platform team policy didn't distinguish shared configs from service-specific ones.

Fix Applied: Rolled back ConfigMap, restarted services. Fixed password encoding.

Lessons Learned:
- Shared ConfigMaps must have validation before deployment (connection test)
- Disable ArgoCD auto-sync for shared infrastructure configs — require manual approval
- Stage shared config changes: deploy to one service first, validate, then roll to others
- Secrets with special characters must be base64 or URL-encoded — add linting
- MTTR of 50 min unacceptable for SEV-1 — create a fast-rollback runbook for shared config
